hints.adpropinquat: 'Adpropinquat' is a finite verb but belongs to the subordinate clause after 'quoniam'
hints.capita: 'Capita' is a noun meaning 'heads' in the accusative plural
hints.redemptio: 'Redemptio' is a noun meaning 'redemption' — subject of the subordinate clause

hints.to lighten: 'To lighten' is 'levare' in a different sense — here 'Levate' means 'to lift/raise'
hints.to relieve: 'To relieve' is 'sublevare' — 'Levate' from 'levare' means 'to raise up'

steps.reveal: Levate = Lift up! Raise! (2nd pl. imperative)

hints.present indicative: Present indicative 2nd plural would be 'levatis' — 'Levate' is imperative
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive 2nd plural would be 'levetis' — 'Levate' is imperative

hints.lego, legere, legi, lectum: These are the parts of 'lego' (to read/choose)
hints.liber, liberare, liberavi, liberatum: 'Libero' (to free) has similar meaning but different parts — note 'libero' not 'liber'
hints.linquo, linquere, liqui, —: These are the parts of 'linquo' (to leave behind)

steps.reveal: adpropinquat = draws near / is approaching (3rd sing. pres.)

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'adpropinquet' — 'adpropinquat' is indicative

hints.propono, proponere, proposui, propositum: These are the parts of 'propono' (to set forth/propose)
hints.approbo, approbare, approbavi, approbatu: These are the parts of 'approbo' (to approve)
hints.propinquo, propinquare, propinquavi, pro: These are the parts of 'propinquo' (to approach) — 'adpropinquare' adds the prefix ad-

hints.nominative plural: 'Capita' looks the same in nominative plural, but here it is the direct object of 'levate'
hints.ablative singular: Ablative singular would be 'capite' — 'capita' is plural
hints.genitive singular: Genitive singular would be 'capitis' — 'capita' ends in -a (neut. pl.)

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'capitis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'capitis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -us — 'capitis' ends in -is

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'redemptionis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'redemptionis' ends in -is
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'redemptionis' ends in -is

steps.prompt: What type of clause does 'quoniam' introduce?
steps.reveal: quoniam adpropinquat = because [your redemption] draws near

hints.result clause (so that): Result clauses use 'ut' — 'quoniam' introduces a causal clause

translation: Lift up your heads, because your redemption draws near
